mnemonics.title: PEMDAS FOR OPERATOR PRECEDENCE
mnemonics.content:
P - Parentheses ()
E - Exponentiation (not in C but in math)
M - Multiplication *
D - Division /
A - Addition +
S - Subtraction -

mnemonics.title: TYPES OF STATEMENTS
mnemonics.content:
S - Selection (if, switch)
I - Iteration (for, while, do-while)
J - Jump (break, continue, return, goto)
E - Expression (assignment, function call)
C - Compound (block with {})

mnemonics.title: UNARY OPERATORS RIGHT-TO-LEFT
mnemonics.content:
ALL UNARY OPERATORS ASSOCIATE RIGHT-TO-LEFT
*ptr++ is parsed as *(ptr++)
++a is same as a = a + 1
-5 is parsed as -(5)

acronyms.expansion: Parentheses, Exponentiation, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction (for remembering operator precedence order)
acronyms.usage: Use to remember which operators are evaluated first in expressions. Note: In C, * and / come before + and -.
An expression that can appear on the left side of an assignment. Must be a modifiable memory location (variable, array element, pointer dereference).
An expression that yields a value but cannot be assigned to. Literal values like 5 or results of expressions like a+b are rvalues.
Describes how operands of operators with same precedence are evaluated. For left-associative operators, last operand is evaluated first in some implementations.

visualTips.visualization: Draw expressions as upside-down trees: root at top is the main operator, branches down are sub-expressions. For a+b*c: + is root, a is left leaf, * is right child with b and c as its leaves.
Think of operator precedence as layers of boxes: innermost (parentheses) is highest, then unary, then multiplicative, then additive. Operators in same layer have equal priority.
Use nested boxes to represent compound statements: outer box is function, inner boxes are if/for/while blocks. Variables declared in inner boxes cannot be accessed outside.
Draw circular arrows: for loop has initialization, condition check, body, update all in one line. while checks condition then goes to body. do-while goes to body then checks condition.

analogies.analogy: An expression is like a recipe ingredient (it has a value). A statement is like a complete instruction or command (it performs an action). Just as you combine ingredients to make a dish, you combine expressions to make statements.
Think of precedence as order of operations in a factory assembly line. Workers (operators) with higher priority get to work first. Multiplication workers arrive before addition workers.
Side effects are like collateral damage in a battle. When you use ++x, you're not just getting a value, you're also changing x's value - the side effect.
In AND (&&), if first condition is false, there's no point checking the second - just like if first domino falls and hits the ground, remaining dominoes won't fall.
A compound statement with braces is like a group WhatsApp chat: multiple individual messages (statements) are bundled together and treated as one unit.
